chapters.title: Fulfilling a mother's dream
chapters.text:
The party is for little Jennette, who is turning six. The living room is full. Her grandparents, three older brothers, her dad, and most of all, her mom, are all there. Mom is at the center of Jennette's world, and the two of them are best friends. Jennette tears the paper off the gift, a two-piece outfit her mom picked out, and she hates it. She cannot bear to disappoint, so she fakes the brightest smile she can and announces it is her favorite. Then comes the birthday wish. Jennette wishes her mom will live for one more year.

Jennette was two when her mom was told she had stage four breast cancer. The cancer went away, but the memory of those years never did. Her mom told the story to anyone who would listen, from relatives to people she met on the street. She would gather her children in a circle and replay old VHS tapes of herself in the hospital, weak and bald. The ritual made Jennette and her brothers squirm, but their mom seemed proud of those painful years.

As Jennette McCurdy has written, the air in the house has felt like a held breath for as long as she can remember, and the fragility of her mom's life has been the center of her own.

Jennette McCurdy

Still, Jennette and her mother shared a bond that felt unbreakable. Jennette had her own quiet mission, to live out the dreams her mom had been forced to set aside. One of those dreams was to act, a chance her grandparents had refused to give their daughter. That is exactly why Jennette's mom wanted more for her girl, to live the life she never had and turn her into Mommy's little actress.

Children who act rarely get to choose their own path, because they sit at the mercy of their parents' plans.

This summary follows the tangled stories of Jennette's childhood, showing a life of bright fame on one side and deep harm on the other.

chapters.title: Child stars lose their childhood
chapters.text:
Jennette's mom was set on making her dream come true. Her little girl would be a big star. Every big star starts small, and so did Jennette. Her first jobs were small background parts in shows and films. Her mom liked to point out the many big sacrifices she had made for Jennette's career, like overcoming her fear of highways and driving two hours to Los Angeles for auditions.

Child actors often live a busy, tiring life, splitting their days between commuting, set work, and schoolwork.

Home was not exactly a refuge. Money was tight. Even with her dad holding down two jobs, the rent was often late. Her parents' marriage was tense, and her mom would often blow up and accuse her dad of not caring for the family. Jennette watched many fights, some of them physical, and many ended with her dad being sent out of the house. She wanted the same closeness with her dad that she had with her mom, but he seemed too far away and too worn out.

The house was often chaotic. After beating cancer, her mom became obsessed with stuff. The rooms filled up floor to ceiling, and the children had to sleep on mats in the living room. Church became Jennette's one place of peace. She loved leaving the house on Sundays. Her Mormon family never missed a service, especially after her mom's diagnosis.

Meanwhile, her acting career was starting to pick up. Jennette showed the two traits every child actor needs, cooperation and submission. But getting noticed by a few directors was not enough for her mom, who wanted to take her daughter's career higher. The famous agent Barbara Cameron agreed to represent Jennette, on one condition, that she attend acting classes.

Jennette hated performing in front of others, especially her mom. Her mom was always there, watching, judging, and pointing out what to fix. Still, Jennette landed her first speaking role, hoping it would finally make her mom happy.

chapters.title: Rising fame, rising problems
chapters.text:
Jennette landed one role after another, getting callbacks for most of the parts she tried out for. The stakes rose when another top manager, Susan Curtis, agreed to represent her. But her growing success was never enough for her mom. Jennette's mom did whatever it took to make the career work, from forcing her to attend auditions with a high fever to coaching her to cry on cue. Crying on cue caused Jennette real pain, because it meant reliving the worst moments of her life. She felt like a trained animal, but it made her stand out as a child actor. Jennette finally told her mom how she really felt. She did not want to act anymore. Her mom burst into tears and shouted, You cannot quit! Jennette's attempt to pitch her own screenplay also failed, because her mom would not let her switch careers.

Other family members noticed Jennette's strain. She deserved to be a kid and have fun, her grandfather kept saying. Jennette wondered what fun even meant. She was not allowed to go outside alone anymore, because her mom had convinced herself someone would kidnap her, the way other child stars had been taken. So Jennette built small rituals at home that her relatives quietly suspected looked like obsessive-compulsive behavior. But Jennette knew the voice in her head was the Holy Ghost, telling her what to do to make sure she would succeed.

Working hard and earning money for the whole family puts a lot of unhealthy pressure on a child.

On top of the tension, Jennette never felt comfortable in her body. Her mom worried about Jennette's looks and decided her natural beauty needed adjusting. Store-bought makeup became a regular fix. For some roles, her appearance was not ethereal enough, and for others she was too pretty. Her mom had full control over Jennette's body, and she was not allowed to shower alone until she turned sixteen.

Jennette believed she had to stay her mom's little girl. The first signs of growing breasts scared her, so she asked her mom for advice on how to stay small. The answer was clear. Eat less. Her mom survived on steamed vegetables and granola bars and taught Jennette all the tricks for keeping weight down. Jennette heard people around her talk about anorexia without understanding what the word meant. It did not matter. She had hit the big time. Jennette had booked iCarly.

chapters.title: iCarly: fame, anorexia, and harassment
chapters.text:
An iCarly role would finally be enough to make Mom happy, right? Jennette was now getting show-sponsored baskets overflowing with food, hundred-dollar gift cards, and other small gifts. Miranda Cosgrove, the lead, was unusually kind to her. But her mom warned Jennette to stay away from Miranda, claiming she listened to dangerous music and had no faith in God. Despite the warnings, Jennette signed up for AIM, a messenger where she and Miranda talked for hours and became close friends.

Building friendships inside the entertainment world can take some weight off the industry.

The Creator behind the show was a hard taskmaster. He wanted everything done his way. Jennette was told to film in a bikini as a child, and to have her first on-screen kiss. She felt deeply uncomfortable. She had no wish to look or act in a sexual way, but the choice was never hers. The Creator could flip from friendly to terrifying, insulting and humiliating anyone nearby. It was safer not to make him angry. That is why Jennette stayed quiet when he offered her a drink as a minor or when he gave her a massage. He rewarded her compliance by promising her own show in a few years. Whatever the price, it made her mom happy.

As the iCarly paycheck brought more stability, her mom finally relaxed a little. The collector calls stopped and the bills went out on time. Jennette's body also behaved, since she stuck to her strict diet. Unfortunately, she started breaking out and got her first period. Because of that, she had to push harder to keep anorexia in place and stay a kid. Growing up was out of the question.

At the same time, the endless talk shows, red carpets, and strangers on the street became the next source of stress. People recognized her everywhere she went, and the pressure kept piling up. She felt lonelier by the day. And, with her career taking off and her mom's health holding steady, the family had stopped going to church.

As Jennette McCurdy has written, who needs God when you have clean mammograms and a series regular role on Nickelodeon.

Jennette McCurdy

At times, Jennette felt she hated her mom, a feeling that was new to her. She had not chosen this life, her mom had. Her mom's dream had come true, but the cost was Jennette's own happiness.

chapters.title: Finding freedom in distance
chapters.text:
Jennette was closing in on eighteen. iCarly was on a short break, and her mom decided it was time to push her daughter's music career, the way every teen actor tried back then. Things seemed to be moving along until a painful truth resurfaced. Her mom's cancer had come back. Jennette had to go on her first tour on her own.

Being away from her mother for the first time gave Jennette a rush of freedom and a shot at real growth.

On the road, Jennette realized how draining it had been to keep tweaking herself for her mom's approval. Her habits shifted too. She started eating a lot. Without her mom watching and cutting her off, Jennette enjoyed big meals and all kinds of food. But a deep wave of guilt came with every bite. The pounds she gained were the first thing her mom clocked when Jennette came home.

Her mom was not getting better. She had lost weight and her hair and was now in a wheelchair. Even as her health slipped, she kept trying to steer her daughter's life. Just as Jennette was about to step into her own apartment, the plan fell apart. Her mom sweetly asked to stay one night, and she never left.

Spending so many hours on set brought Jennette close to one of her co-workers, Joe, a man in his thirties. He broke up with his girlfriend to be with Jennette, a relationship her mom would never have approved of. After more than a year together, they were still hiding it. When her mom moved back to California for cancer treatment, Jennette found it easier to keep the relationship quiet, even though she called her mom ten times a day.

A show-sponsored trip to Hawaii changed everything. Paparazzi snapped them together the moment Jennette let her guard down. The photos were online within an hour, followed by a flood of missed calls and angry messages from her mother.

Her mom called her ugly monster and devil child, and threatened that the family would cut her off. She went as far as posting gossip about Jennette in fan groups, asking fans to turn their backs on her.

Jennette wanted to talk it through, but her mom never brought the topic up in person, pretending nothing had happened. After that, their calls shrank to polite, surface-level small talk.

chapters.title: Mom's death and what came after
chapters.text:
Later that year, Jennette threw a birthday dinner for her mom. Everything looked fine until Jennette saw her mom convulsing. A brain tumor had triggered a seizure, and her mom lay in the ICU, unresponsive for a week. Jennette spent long hours at the hospital every day. Burger King was the fastest lunch nearby, and the moment her mom came out of the coma, she told Jennette she should not be eating anything with that much fat in it.

Her mom's condition pulled Jennette out of binging, and she started losing weight fast. At the same time, she got friendly with alcohol. It made her worries vanish, at least for a while. Her mom was in hospice care for weeks, until her dad called Jennette to come down for that day. She watched her mom take her last breath. News outlets ran the story a minute later.

Jennette began pouring all of her grief into her eating disorder. She was determined to keep going, in honor of her mom. Bulimia became her new escape hatch. Even if she slipped and ate the wrong thing, she could undo it. She tried all kinds of tricks to keep food out, like spraying perfume on her burgers, but nothing worked as well as bulimia. She got more compliments on her looks than ever. The more miserable she felt about her body, the more praise she got for it.

Eating disorders can act as a way to feel in control of life by controlling the body.

Jennette's work life was not satisfying either. The Creator had dangled the promise of her own show years before, but now she was stuck co-starring with Ariana Grande in Sam and Cat. With her music career on the rise, Ariana often skipped the shoot. Jennette grew more and more angry about being stuck in the mess. She hated her Sam character. She kept going because the spin-off let her direct a few episodes. It did not take long to realize the promise of her own show was another industry lie.

Jennette counted the days until the show wrapped, and then a story broke. Nickelodeon was dealing with emotional abuse allegations against the Creator. Jennette got a call, offering her three hundred thousand dollars to stay quiet about her experiences with the Creator. She turned the money down without thinking. In the end, another sexual harassment claim shut the show down.

chapters.title: A long and uneven road to healing
chapters.text:
Soon after the show ended, Netflix offered Jennette a starring role. She met Steven on set, and the relationship mattered a great deal to her. He cared about her in a real way and took her bulimia seriously. She did not want to lose him, so seeing a therapist became the only option. Laura was ready to give Jennette the emotional support she needed to deal with her eating disorder.

But therapy scared Jennette. It was the only way to face her drinking and bulimia head on. One of the sessions eventually led to her mom. Was she a good mother, or was she an abuser? Questioning her mother's authority was unbearable, and Jennette quit therapy on the spot.

The personal drama did not end there. Her usually distant father was doing surprisingly well since her mom had passed, and Jennette wondered why. One day, he gathered the children to share the secret their mom had never shared. He was not their biological father. Years before, their mom had an affair with a trombone player who worked on soundtracks for Hollywood films and shows.

Everyone was stunned. Why had their mom never told them before she died? Jennette found her biological father at one of the jazz clubs where he played, and he recognized her right away. She learned the truth. Their mom had lied to the court, claiming he was physically abusive, and won a custody battle when the kids were small.

Finding out that a parent was not who they seemed forces a person to rewrite the story of their relationships and childhood.

At the same time, Jennette was reaching a breaking point with her bulimia. Losing a tooth was the final push, and she turned to Jeff, an eating disorder specialist. Jeff learned about her food habits and was determined to help her eat normally again. The progress was slow but steady. As she got better, Steven got worse. His recent schizophrenia diagnosis meant he had to take medication, and he was already heavily dependent on weed. Jennette tried to help him, wanting to give back what he had given her, and the two of them fell into a deeply codependent pattern. In the end, she realized breaking up was the only way out.

Jennette was starting to rediscover what she really wanted. She took an open-ended break from acting, made her mental health a priority, and decided to write again. The time gave her space to rethink her relationship with her mother and the childhood she had never had, shaped by her mom's deeply controlling and abusive behavior. Jennette could finally see her mom for who she really was.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Years after Jennette's mom died of cancer, the young woman began to look again at the hidden story of her childhood. Forced to work as a child actress from the age of six, Jennette did everything she could to make her mom's dream come true. She tried over and over to meet her mom's standards, but she could never quite reach them. At the same time, she never got the chance to explore her own wishes as a child.

From teaching her daughter an eating disorder at a young age to making her work to support the whole family, her mom was a deeply manipulative, abusive, and controlling person. Stuck in a career she hated, Jennette felt exposed and unprotected against her mother. The industry was happy to take advantage of her, no matter how violated she felt.

After years of therapy, Jennette saw clearly how putting her mom on a pedestal had hurt her well-being and self-worth. She could not get those years back, but learning the truth gave her the power to choose her own path. Her recovery has been hard, and she is glad she started it.

A small practice can carry these ideas forward. It can help to look back at the history of your own childhood, and to hear the stories of others who have done the same. For a deeper look at Jennette's reflections, her podcast Empty Inside takes on uncomfortable topics with different guests, and her one-woman show I'm Glad My Mom Died has played at theaters in Los Angeles.
